what_it_measures: Protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen throughout the body.
Percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells.
Total number of red blood cells per unit volume of blood.
Total number of white blood cells; indicates immune system activity and infection.
Number of platelets; essential for blood clotting and wound healing.
Average size of red blood cells; helps classify type of anemia.
Average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell.
Concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of red blood cells.
Variation in red blood cell size; elevated in nutritional deficiencies and mixed anemias.
Direct measure of red cell size variation in fL (standard deviation).
Average size of platelets; larger platelets are more metabolically active.
Variation in platelet size; indicates platelet activation or destruction.
Immature red blood cells; reflects bone marrow activity and red blood cell production rate.
Percentage of neutrophils in WBC; primary defense against bacterial infections.
Absolute number of neutrophils; critical marker for infection risk and immune status.
Percentage of lymphocytes; key cells for adaptive immunity against viral infections.
Absolute number of lymphocytes; decreased in HIV, immunosuppression, or severe infection.
Percentage of monocytes; involved in chronic inflammation and immune regulation.
Percentage of eosinophils; elevated in allergic reactions and parasitic infections.
Percentage of basophils; involved in allergic and inflammatory responses.
Immature neutrophils released during severe infection; left shift indicator.
Absolute monocyte count; elevated in chronic infections and inflammatory diseases.
Absolute eosinophil count; used to monitor allergy, asthma, and parasitic disease.
Absolute number of basophils in the blood; elevated in allergic reactions and myeloproliferative disorders.
Blood sugar level after at least 8 hours of fasting; primary diabetes screening test.
Blood glucose at any time of day regardless of last meal.
Blood glucose 2 hours after a meal; tests how well the body manages glucose after eating.
Average blood sugar over 2-3 months; gold standard for diabetes monitoring.
Insulin level after fasting; used to assess insulin resistance and pancreatic function.
Byproduct of insulin production; distinguishes type 1 from type 2 diabetes.
Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance; quantifies insulin resistance.
Blood glucose 1 hour after a 75g glucose load; screens for gestational diabetes.
Average blood sugar over 2-3 weeks; used when HbA1c is unreliable.
Waste product from muscle metabolism filtered by kidneys; elevated in kidney disease.
Nitrogen from urea in blood; reflects protein metabolism and kidney filtration.
End product of purine metabolism; elevated levels cause gout and kidney stones.
Estimated glomerular filtration rate; best overall measure of kidney function.
Urea concentration in blood; reflects protein catabolism and kidney excretion.
Ratio used to distinguish pre-renal from renal causes of elevated BUN.
Protein produced by all cells; more accurate than creatinine for early kidney disease detection.
Creatinine in urine; used to assess kidney function and validate 24-hour urine collections.
Small amounts of albumin in urine; earliest marker of diabetic kidney disease.
Protein-to-creatinine ratio in urine; screens for proteinuria without 24-hour collection.
Small protein found on cell surfaces; elevated in kidney disease, multiple myeloma, and lymphoma.
Liver enzyme released when liver cells are damaged; most specific marker for liver injury.
Enzyme present in liver, heart, and muscles; elevated in liver disease, heart attack, or muscle injury.
Enzyme from liver, bone, and kidneys; elevated in liver disease, bile duct obstruction, or bone disorders.
Liver enzyme elevated in alcohol use, bile duct disease, and drug-induced liver damage.
Total bilirubin from red blood cell breakdown; elevated in jaundice, liver disease, or hemolysis.
Water-soluble bilirubin processed by liver; elevated in bile duct obstruction and liver disease.
Pre-liver bilirubin; elevated in hemolytic anemia or Gilbert syndrome.
Main protein made by liver; low levels indicate malnutrition, liver disease, or protein loss.
Total albumin and globulin in blood; reflects nutritional status and liver/immune function.
Ratio of albumin to globulin; low ratio suggests chronic liver disease or multiple myeloma.
Time for blood to clot via extrinsic pathway; prolonged in liver disease or vitamin K deficiency.
Standardized measure of blood clotting time; used to monitor warfarin therapy.
Ratio used to differentiate alcoholic liver disease (>2) from viral hepatitis (<1).
Total blood cholesterol; values above 200 mg/dL increase cardiovascular risk.
Low-density lipoprotein; bad cholesterol that builds up in arteries and increases heart disease risk.
High-density lipoprotein; good cholesterol that removes other cholesterol from arteries.
Blood fat from diet and liver; elevated levels increase cardiovascular and pancreatitis risk.
Very low-density lipoprotein; carries triglycerides and contributes to arterial plaque.
Cardiovascular risk ratio; higher ratio indicates greater risk of heart disease.
All cholesterol except HDL; better predictor of cardiovascular risk than LDL alone.
Main protein in HDL particles; reflects HDL function and cardiovascular protection.
Protein in LDL and VLDL; reflects number of atherogenic particles causing plaque.
Genetically determined lipoprotein; independent risk factor for heart disease and stroke.
Pituitary hormone that controls thyroid function; first-line thyroid screening test.
Unbound T4 hormone available to body tissues; primary thyroid hormone for metabolism regulation.
Active form of thyroid hormone; regulates metabolism, heart rate, and energy production.
Total T4 including protein-bound; reflects overall thyroid output.
Total T3 including protein-bound; most potent thyroid hormone affecting metabolism.
Antibody against thyroid enzyme; elevated in Hashimoto thyroiditis and Graves disease.
Antibody against thyroglobulin; elevated in autoimmune thyroid disease.
Protein made exclusively by thyroid; used to monitor thyroid cancer treatment.
Inactive form of T3; elevated in chronic illness, stress, and caloric restriction.
Main extracellular electrolyte; regulates fluid balance, blood pressure, and nerve function.
Main intracellular electrolyte; critical for heart rhythm, muscle contraction, and nerve function.
Main anion in blood; maintains fluid balance, blood pressure, and acid-base status.
Main blood buffer; low levels indicate metabolic acidosis, high levels indicate alkalosis.
Essential mineral for bone health, muscle contraction, nerve function, and blood clotting.
Metabolically active free calcium; more accurate than total calcium for assessing hypocalcemia.
Cofactor in 300+ enzyme reactions; essential for muscle, nerve, and heart function.
Mineral for bone and energy metabolism; regulated by parathyroid hormone and kidneys.
Difference between measured cations and anions; elevated in metabolic acidosis.
Storage form of vitamin D; essential for calcium absorption, bone health, and immune function.
Essential vitamin for nerve function, DNA synthesis, and red blood cell production.
B vitamin essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and prevention of neural tube defects.
Essential for carbohydrate metabolism and nerve function; deficiency causes beriberi.
Cofactor in amino acid metabolism; important for neurotransmitter synthesis and immune function.
Fat-soluble vitamin for vision, immune function, and cell growth.
Antioxidant vitamin protecting cells from oxidative damage; supports immune function.
Antioxidant essential for collagen synthesis, wound healing, and immune defense.
Essential trace element for immune function, wound healing, and DNA synthesis.
Trace mineral for iron metabolism, nerve function, and antioxidant enzyme activity.
Antioxidant trace element essential for thyroid function and immune defense.
Trace mineral for enzyme function, bone development, and antioxidant defense.
Circulating iron bound to transferrin; reflects current iron availability for red blood cell production.
Iron storage protein; most sensitive marker of iron deficiency before anemia develops.
Maximum iron-carrying capacity of transferrin; elevated in iron deficiency anemia.
Protein that transports iron in blood; elevated in iron deficiency, decreased in chronic disease.
Percentage of transferrin binding sites occupied by iron; low in deficiency, high in overload.
Available iron-binding capacity on transferrin; part of iron panel to assess stores.
Primary male sex hormone; regulates libido, muscle mass, bone density, and mood.
Biologically active testosterone not bound to proteins; more clinically relevant than total.
Main female estrogen; regulates menstrual cycle, bone density, and reproductive function.
Hormone that prepares uterus for pregnancy; low in menopause, high in luteal phase.
Pituitary hormone triggering ovulation in women and testosterone production in men.
Pituitary hormone regulating egg and sperm development; elevated in menopause.
Pituitary hormone that stimulates milk production; elevated causes menstrual irregularities.
Adrenal androgen precursor; declines with age; marker of adrenal function.
Stress hormone from adrenal cortex; regulates metabolism, immune response, and blood pressure.
Adrenal hormone regulating sodium retention and blood pressure; elevated in hyperaldosteronism.
Hormone regulating calcium and phosphorus; elevated in hyperparathyroidism or vitamin D deficiency.
Growth factor mediating effects of growth hormone; used to diagnose growth disorders.
Pituitary hormone stimulating growth and cell reproduction; elevated in acromegaly.
Cardiac-specific protein released during heart muscle damage; gold standard for myocardial infarction.
Cardiac muscle protein; elevated within 3 hours of heart attack.
Heart muscle enzyme; elevated 4-6 hours after myocardial infarction.
Total creatine kinase from heart, muscle, and brain; elevated in muscle injury or heart attack.
Heart failure hormone; elevated proportionally to degree of ventricular dysfunction.
N-terminal fragment of BNP; more stable marker for heart failure diagnosis and monitoring.
Oxygen-binding protein in heart and skeletal muscle; earliest marker after muscle injury.
Amino acid metabolite; elevated levels independently increase cardiovascular and stroke risk.
Liver protein rising rapidly in response to infection, inflammation, or tissue injury.
Ultra-sensitive CRP measuring cardiovascular inflammation risk at very low levels.
Rate of red blood cell settling; non-specific marker of systemic inflammation.
Acute phase reactant elevated in inflammation, infection, and iron overload conditions.
Pro-inflammatory cytokine; elevated in sepsis, autoimmune disease, and COVID-19.
Precursor of calcitonin; specific marker for bacterial sepsis and guides antibiotic therapy.
Clotting factor and acute phase reactant; elevated in inflammation and increases thrombosis risk.
Enzyme released from damaged cells; elevated in tissue injury, hemolysis, and various cancers.
Tests intrinsic clotting pathway; prolonged in hemophilia and during heparin therapy.
Fibrin degradation product; elevated in DVT, pulmonary embolism, DIC, and COVID-19.
Final step of clotting; prolonged in fibrinogen deficiency and heparin anticoagulation.
Screens for platelet dysfunction and von Willebrand disease.
Coagulation factor; deficient in Hemophilia A; elevated in thrombosis risk.
Natural anticoagulant inhibiting thrombin; deficiency increases clotting risk.
Natural anticoagulant protein; deficiency causes hypercoagulability and recurrent thrombosis.
Cofactor for Protein C; deficiency predisposes to venous thromboembolism.
Autoantibodies against nuclear components; screening test for systemic autoimmune diseases.
Antibody to double-stranded DNA; highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
Autoantibody against IgG; elevated in rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune diseases.
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide; most specific early marker for rheumatoid arthritis.
Central complement protein; decreased in active lupus and immune complex diseases.
Complement component consumed in immune complex diseases; low in hereditary angioedema.
Highly specific autoantibody for SLE; found in 25-30% of lupus patients.
Antibody against neutrophil granule proteins; marker for vasculitis (Wegener, microscopic polyangiitis).
Protein from prostate gland; primary screening marker for prostate cancer.
Glycoprotein elevated in colorectal, lung, breast, and GI cancers; used for monitoring treatment.
Fetal protein elevated in liver cancer, testicular cancer, and neural tube defects.
Glycoprotein elevated in ovarian cancer; also used for monitoring endometriosis.
Carbohydrate antigen elevated in pancreatic, bile duct, and GI cancers.
Glycoprotein elevated in breast cancer; primarily used to monitor treatment response.
In non-pregnant context, elevated in testicular, ovarian, and gestational trophoblastic tumors.
Enzyme from neuroendocrine cells; elevated in small cell lung cancer and neuroblastoma.
Cytokeratin fragment elevated in non-small cell lung cancer; used for monitoring.
Surface protein of hepatitis B virus; positive indicates active or chronic hepatitis B infection.
Protective antibody; levels above 10 mIU/mL indicate immunity to hepatitis B.
Antibody to hepatitis C virus; reactive result requires confirmatory RNA testing.
Combined 4th-generation HIV test detecting both p24 antigen and antibodies to HIV-1 and HIV-2.
HIV RNA copies in blood; monitors treatment effectiveness; undetectable = untransmittable.
Non-treponemal test screening for syphilis; reactive requires confirmatory treponemal testing.
Agglutination test for typhoid fever antibodies; titer above 1:160 suggests active infection.
Dengue viral antigen detectable in early infection (days 1-5) before antibody response.
Detects Plasmodium antigens in blood; rapid screening for malaria in febrile patients.
Acid-base status of urine; affected by diet, medications, and urinary tract conditions.
Concentration of dissolved substances in urine; reflects hydration status and kidney concentrating ability.
Protein detected in urine; elevated in kidney disease, hypertension, and diabetes.
Glucose in urine; normally absent; present when blood glucose exceeds renal threshold in diabetes.
Fat breakdown products in urine; elevated in diabetic ketoacidosis, fasting, and ketogenic diet.
Red blood cells in urine; elevated in UTI, kidney stones, glomerulonephritis, or tumors.
White blood cells in urine; elevated in urinary tract infections and kidney inflammation.
Bacterial conversion product; positive result indicates gram-negative bacterial urinary tract infection.
Conjugated bilirubin in urine; normally absent; elevated in obstructive jaundice and hepatitis.
Ovarian reserve marker; decreasing levels indicate diminishing egg supply.
Protein binding sex hormones; high levels reduce free testosterone bioavailability.
Human chorionic gonadotropin; confirms pregnancy; doubles every 48-72 hours in early pregnancy.
Estrogen predominant after menopause; produced mainly in adipose tissue from androgens.
Glycoprotein from gonads; suppresses FSH; marker of ovarian reserve and spermatogenesis.
Digestive enzyme from pancreas and salivary glands; elevated in pancreatitis.
Pancreatic enzyme breaking down fat; more specific than amylase for acute pancreatitis.
Tumor marker for pancreatic cancer; also elevated in cholangiocarcinoma and biliary obstruction.
Bone formation marker produced by osteoblasts; indicates bone turnover rate.
Bone-specific enzyme from osteoblasts; marker of bone formation used in metabolic bone disease.
Collagen breakdown product; marker of bone resorption used to monitor osteoporosis treatment.
Collagen cross-link marker of bone resorption; elevated in osteoporosis and bone metastases.
Active form of vitamin D synthesized in kidneys; directly stimulates calcium absorption.
Most abundant antibody class providing long-term immunity; deficiency causes recurrent infections.
Antibody protecting mucosal surfaces; deficiency increases susceptibility to respiratory and GI infections.
First antibody produced in immune response; elevated in acute infections and Waldenström macroglobulinemia.
Antibody mediating allergic responses; elevated in atopy, allergic diseases, and parasitic infections.
Helper T-cells coordinating immune response; below 200 cells/µL defines AIDS in HIV infection.
Cytotoxic T-cells killing virus-infected cells; used with CD4 to assess HIV immune status.
Total IgE level; elevated indicates allergic tendency, atopy, or parasitic infection.
Mast cell enzyme; elevated immediately after anaphylaxis or in systemic mastocytosis.
Protein released by activated eosinophils; marker for allergic inflammation and asthma severity.
Product of anaerobic metabolism; elevated in sepsis, shock, and tissue hypoxia.
Toxic byproduct of protein metabolism; elevated in liver failure and causes hepatic encephalopathy.
Sensitive marker for vitamin B12 functional deficiency; elevated before anemia develops.
Vascular inflammation enzyme; elevated in coronary artery disease and stroke risk.
Anti-inflammatory adipokine; low levels associated with insulin resistance, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
Satiety hormone from fat cells; elevated in obesity; low in anorexia and lipodystrophy.
Acid-base status of arterial blood; below 7.35 is acidosis, above 7.45 is alkalosis.
Carbon dioxide tension in arterial blood; reflects respiratory acid-base control.
Oxygen tension in arterial blood; reflects lung efficiency in oxygenating blood.
Percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen; below 90% is hypoxemia requiring intervention.
Metabolic component of acid-base balance; decreased in metabolic acidosis.
Measure of metabolic acid-base component; negative in metabolic acidosis, positive in alkalosis.
Blood level of digoxin cardiac glycoside; narrow therapeutic window requiring monitoring.
Blood lithium concentration; therapeutic range for bipolar disorder; narrow toxic threshold.
Anticonvulsant blood level; requires monitoring to prevent toxicity and seizure breakthrough.
Pre-dose vancomycin antibiotic level; guides dosing to optimize efficacy and prevent nephrotoxicity.
Paracetamol blood level; critical in overdose assessment to guide N-acetylcysteine treatment.
Aspirin metabolite level; elevated in toxicity causing tinnitus, acidosis, and altered consciousness.
Blood alcohol concentration; above 80 mg/dL impairs driving; above 300 mg/dL life-threatening.
Lead in blood; neurotoxic at any level in children; causes irreversible developmental damage.
Short half-life protein reflecting recent nutritional status; better acute indicator than albumin.
Carrier protein for vitamin A; short half-life makes it sensitive marker of nutritional status.
Iron transport protein; used as nutritional marker; decreased in malnutrition and protein loss.
Water-soluble B vitamin; deficiency causes pellagra; involved in energy metabolism.
Coenzyme A precursor essential for fat and carbohydrate metabolism.
B vitamin supporting carboxylase enzymes; deficiency causes hair loss and dermatitis.
Antioxidant in mitochondrial energy production; low in statin users and heart failure.
Percentage of EPA and DHA in red blood cell membranes; reflects cardiovascular protection.
Brain protein; elevated after traumatic brain injury and used as marker of neurological damage.
Astrocyte protein; elevated in traumatic brain injury, stroke, and neurodegenerative diseases.
Neuronal damage marker; elevated in multiple sclerosis, ALS, Alzheimer, and traumatic brain injury.
Neuronal microtubule protein; elevated in Alzheimer disease and other neurodegenerative conditions.
CSF amyloid peptide; low levels indicate amyloid plaque deposition in Alzheimer disease.
Lectin mediating fibrosis; elevated in heart failure, liver fibrosis, and inflammation.
Innate immunity protein; more sensitive than CRP for vascular inflammation and sepsis.
Anti-aging protein regulating calcium, phosphorus, and fibroblast growth factor; declines with age.
Pro-inflammatory cytokine elevated in chronic age-related inflammation and metabolic syndrome.
Inflammatory cytokine mediating systemic inflammation; elevated in sepsis, arthritis, and cachexia.
Biological aging marker; shorter telomeres correlate with accelerated aging and disease risk.
Lead toxicity screening in children; no safe level established; impairs neurodevelopment.
Amino acid accumulating in PKU; newborn screening prevents intellectual disability with early treatment.
Newborn thyroid screening; early detection of congenital hypothyroidism prevents intellectual disability.
Adrenal steroid precursor; elevated in congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency).
Metabolite screened in newborns; elevated in galactosemia causing liver disease and cataracts.
Pituitary hormone stimulating cortisol production; elevated in Addison disease, low in Cushing syndrome.
Enzyme initiating renin-angiotensin system; regulates blood pressure and aldosterone release.
Catecholamine metabolites; markedly elevated in pheochromocytoma adrenal tumor.
Enzyme in red blood cells; inhibited by organophosphate pesticides; used in toxicity assessment.
Protease inhibitor protecting lungs and liver; deficiency causes emphysema and liver cirrhosis.
Copper-carrying protein in blood; low in Wilson disease, a hereditary copper metabolism disorder.
Protein binding free hemoglobin; low in hemolytic anemia, elevated in inflammation.
IgG subclass; elevated in IgG4-related disease affecting pancreas, bile ducts, and salivary glands.
Antimicrobial enzyme from monocytes; elevated in sarcoidosis and monocytic leukemia.
Inflammatory enzyme releasing arachidonic acid; elevated in pancreatitis and sepsis.
Enzyme converting angiotensin; elevated in sarcoidosis; inhibited by ACE inhibitor medications.
Neuropeptide from gut and nervous system; elevated in VIPoma causing severe watery diarrhea.
Protein from neuroendocrine cells; elevated in carcinoid tumors, pheochromocytoma, and neuroendocrine tumors.
Stomach hormone stimulating acid secretion; markedly elevated in Zollinger-Ellison syndrome.
Antidiuretic hormone from pituitary; regulates water retention; elevated in SIADH, low in diabetes insipidus.
